figures.caption: Figure 4: Mean relative rollout errors per step on test set (solid line), with standard deviation (lighter area). Relative rollout error grows linearly. Standard deviation increases as time progresses.

Figure 5: Rollout results after 1000 steps. First row: correct solution (left), prediction (middle), pointwise error(right) for parameters µ1 = 0.0206, µ2 = 0.544, µ3 = 0.428. Second row: correct solution (left), prediction (middle), pointwise error (right) for parameters µ1 = 0.0264, µ2 = 0.528, µ3 = 0.553.

Figure 6: Mean relative rollout error (solid line) with standard deviation (shaded area) on the test set over time for velocities ux (left), uy (middle), and pressure (right). Relative rollout error grows linearly for velocities ux, uy but has periodic spikes for pressure. Standard deviation increases as time progresses. Unlike velocity, pressure in incompressible flow is determined globally through a Poisson equation, making it more sensitive to phase errors in the periodic shedding cycle. A small phase drift in the predicted vortex positions leads to large pointwise pressure errors at shedding events, even when the overall flow structure is well captured.

Figure 7: Prediction results. Reference solution (left column), network prediction (middle column), and pointwise error (right column) for Re = 1.557e + 03, A = 1.9489e − 01, xc = 1.019, yc = 5.637e − 01, r = 7.772e − 02 at time T = 6.75. Velocity in the x−direction is in the first row, velocity in the y−direction is in the second row, and pressure is in the third row.

Figure 3: Basic overview of the model. The input is solution snapshot at time ϕt, which is passed through convolutional encoder, then split by patches, fed through transformer encoder, whose output is then concatenated into tensor and decoded via convolutional decoder to output predicted ϕt+∆t. Parameters can be injected via multi-layer perceptron into encoder, transformer and decoder via affine modulations.
Figure 1: ResNet block with parameter injection. Parameters are passed through MLP and injected into the ResNet block through affine modulation after the first convolution in the residual block.

Figure 2: Coordinate encoding. Spatial Fourier features are constructed by applying sinusoidal functions at multiple frequencies to the coordinate grid (x, y). The resulting 2k channels per axis are concatenated with the original feature map, yielding an augmented tensor of size H ×W × (C + 4k), where H and W , denote grid height and width and C is the number of solution components.
